perturbation.original_text: Consultant's relationship to Aduro shall be that of an independent contractor. Consultant shall be responsible for the timely payment of his or her own self-employment and income taxes. Neither party shall have any authority to bind the other.
perturbation.changed_text: Consultant's relationship to Aduro shall be that of an independent contractor. Aduro shall withhold and remit all applicable income taxes on behalf of Consultant. Neither party shall have any authority to bind the other.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that Aduro will withhold and remit income taxes, the agreement now contradicts the definition of an independent contractor, who is responsible for their own taxes. This blurs the lines between independent contractor and employee.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 3353 defines an independent contractor as someone who is not subject to the control of an employer regarding the means by which they achieve a specific result, and this arrangement typically means that the independent contractor is responsible for their own tax obligations. By stating that Aduro will withhold taxes, it suggests an employer-employee relationship, conflicting with the independent contractor designation.

WORKERS' COMPENSATION AND INSURANCE [3200 - 6002] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1979, Ch. 373. ) PART 1. SCOPE AND OPERATION [3200 - 4418] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employers, Employees, and Dependents [3300 - 3553] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 2. Employees [3350 - 3371.1] ( Article 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 3353. “Independent contractor” means any person who renders service for a specified recompense for a specified result, under the control of his principal as to the result of his work only and not as to the means by which such result is accomplished. (Enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the legal definition of an independent contractor. The original text correctly assigns tax responsibility to the consultant, aligning with their independent status. However, the changed text shifts this responsibility to Aduro. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Labor Code Section 3353, defines an "independent contractor" as someone whose work is controlled by the principal only as to the result, not the means, and implies that the independent contractor manages their own tax obligations. By assuming the responsibility to withhold and remit income taxes, Aduro exercises a level of control typically associated with an employer-employee relationship, directly contradicting the independent contractor designation as it relates to tax responsibility, thus creating a legal contradiction as tax responsibility is a key aspect of the 'means' by which an independent contractor operates.

perturbation.original_text: Aduro shall be the sole and exclusive owner of, and Consultant hereby assigns to Aduro, any and all writings, documents, work product, inventions, developments, improvements, discoveries, know-how, processes, chemical entities, compounds, plans, memoranda, tests, research, designs, specifications, models and data that Consultant makes, conceives, discovers or develops, either solely or jointly with any other person in performance of the Services (collectively, "Work Product").
perturbation.changed_text: Aduro shall be the sole and exclusive owner of, and Consultant hereby assigns to Aduro, any and all writings, documents, work product, inventions, developments, improvements, discoveries, know-how, processes, chemical entities, compounds, plans, memoranda, tests, research, designs, specifications, models and data that Consultant makes, conceives, discovers or develops, either solely or jointly with any other person in performance of the Services (collectively, "Work Product"), excluding any inventions made outside of the specific instructions from Aduro.
perturbation.explanation: By excluding inventions made outside specific instructions, the contract attempts to narrow the definition of 'Work Product'. California law provides protections for employee inventions developed on their own time and not directly related to the employer's business, regardless of contract language. This addition contradicts the general assignment provision and creates ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 2870 protects an employee's rights to inventions they develop entirely on their own time without using the employer's equipment, supplies, facilities, or trade secret information, unless the invention relates to the employer's business or actual or demonstrably anticipated research or development. While the original clause complies with this law, the exclusion added opens the door to a potential conflict by creating an ambiguous category of inventions related to instructions from Aduro, as opposed to the business generally. This attempts to circumvent the protections afforded to independent inventions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, LAB 2870 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Labor Code - LAB DIVISION 3. EMPLOYMENT RELATIONS [2700 - 3122.4] ( Division 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employer and Employee [2750 - 2930] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 3.5. Inventions Made by an Employee [2870 - 2872] ( Article 3.5 added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 1001. ) 2870. (a) Any provision in an employment agreement which provides that an employee shall assign, or offer to assign, any of his or her rights in an invention to his or her employer shall not apply to an invention that the employee developed entirely on his or her own time without using the employer’s equipment, supplies, facilities, or trade secret information except for those inventions that either: (1) Relate at the time of conception or reduction to practice of the invention to the employer’s business, or actual or demonstrably anticipated research or development of the employer; or (2) Result from any work performed by the employee for the employer. (b) To the extent a provision in an employment agreement purports to require an employee to assign an invention otherwise excluded from being required to be assigned under subdivision (a), the provision is against the public policy of this state and is unenforceable. (Amended by Stats. 1991, Ch. 647, Sec. 5.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces an exclusion to the definition of 'Work Product' that contradicts California Labor Code Section 2870. The original text assigns all work product to Aduro. The changed text excludes inventions made outside specific instructions from Aduro, creating a potential conflict with employee invention rights. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically LAB 2870(a), states that an employment agreement cannot require assignment of inventions developed on the employee's own time without using the employer's resources, except when the invention relates to the employer's business or results from work performed for the employer. The added exclusion attempts to narrow the scope of assigned inventions, potentially circumventing the employee protections afforded by Section 2870(a) regarding inventions made on the employee's own time but still related to the employer's business, creating a legal contradiction as defined by Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: From the Effective Date and for twelve (12) months after the termination of this Agreement (the "Restricted Period"), Consultant shall not, without Aduro's prior written consent, directly or indirectly, solicit or encourage any employee or contractor of Aduro or its affiliates to terminate employment with, or cease providing Services to, Aduro or its affiliates.
perturbation.changed_text: From the Effective Date and for twelve (12) months after the termination of this Agreement (the "Restricted Period"), Consultant shall not, without Aduro's prior written consent, directly or indirectly, solicit or encourage any employee or contractor of Aduro or its affiliates to terminate employment with, or cease providing Services to, Aduro or its affiliates, regardless of the reason for the termination.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text broadens the non-solicitation clause by removing consideration of the reasons for termination of employment or service. California law has limitations on non-solicitation agreements, especially when they unduly restrict an individual's ability to practice their profession. By removing any consideration for the reason of solicitation, this clause may be interpreted as an unlawful restraint of trade.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain anyone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While non-solicitation agreements are sometimes upheld, they must be narrowly tailored. The original clause has a better chance of being seen as tailored, but the modified clause broadens the scope of restriction to any solicitation and is more likely to be viewed as an unlawful restraint on trade or profession, particularly if it affects former clients.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text expands the scope of the non-solicitation agreement, potentially violating California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which generally prohibits contracts that restrain someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. The original clause had a better chance of being seen as narrowly tailored, but the modified clause broadens the restriction and is more likely to be viewed as an unlawful restraint on trade or profession. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The removal of consideration for the reasons of termination broadens the restriction, making it more likely to be seen as an unlawful restraint of trade, thus creating a legal contradiction.
